← Developer Tools

JSON Formatter & Validator

Paste any JSON to format it cleanly, validate its syntax, or minify it for production. Errors are highlighted instantly.

Indent:

What does a JSON formatter do?

A JSON formatter (also called a JSON pretty-printer or JSON beautifier) takes compact or unformatted JSON text and rewrites it with consistent indentation and line breaks, making the structure easy to read and navigate. It is the fastest way to inspect complex nested JSON responses from APIs, config files, or database exports without loading a full code editor.

How to format JSON online

Paste your JSON into the input area and click Format. Choose 2-space or 4-space indentation to match your team's style guide. The output appears immediately with proper indentation. Click Copy to copy the formatted JSON to your clipboard, ready to paste into your editor, documentation or API client.

What is JSON minification?

Minification removes all unnecessary whitespace — spaces, tabs and newlines — from JSON text, producing the smallest possible string that remains valid JSON. Minified JSON is used in production APIs and configuration files where every byte counts. A formatted JSON file can be 20–40% larger than its minified equivalent. Click Minify to collapse your JSON to a single line.

How does JSON validation work?

The validator parses your JSON using JavaScript's built-in JSON.parse() and reports the exact error message if the syntax is invalid. Common JSON errors include: trailing commas after the last property (not allowed in standard JSON, unlike JavaScript), missing quotes around property keys, using single quotes instead of double quotes, and mismatched brackets or braces. The error message pinpoints the exact location of the problem.

JSON vs JavaScript object literals

JSON is a strict subset of JavaScript syntax with a few important differences. JSON requires all keys to be double-quoted strings. JSON does not allow trailing commas after the last item in an array or object. JSON does not support undefined, functions or comments. If you are copying object literals from JavaScript source code and they fail to validate, these are the most likely causes.

Is my JSON data safe?

Yes. All formatting, validation and minification happens locally inside your browser using JavaScript's native JSON.parse() and JSON.stringify() functions. Your data is never sent to any server or stored anywhere.
